concepts.title: Surface Tension
concepts.conceptBody:
Surface Tension

The free surface of a liquid constracts so that its exposed surface area becomes minimum, i.e., it behaves as if it were under tension, somewhat like a stretched elastic membrane. This property is known as surface tension.

The surface tension of a liquid varies with temperature as well as dissolved impurities, etc. When soap is mixed with water, the surface tension of water decreases.

Surfance tension of a liquid is measured by the normal force acting per unit length. On either side of an imaginary line drawn on the free surface of a liquid, the direction of this force is perpendicular to the line and tangential of the free surface of liquid.

 

$\text{T}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{L}}$

Consider a wire frame, as shown in figure, equipped with a sliding wire $\text{AB}$. It is dipped is soapy water. A filmk of liquid is formed. A force $\text{F}$ has to be applied to hold the wire in place. Since, the soap film has two surfaces attached to the wire, the total length of the film is contact with the wire is $2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}$.

$\text{T}\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ surface }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tension }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)=\frac{\text{F}}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}$

Properties of surface Tension:

  • Scalar quantity
  • Temperature sensitive
  • Impurity sensitive
  • Depends only on the nature of the liquid
  • Unit of surface tension, N/m
  • Dimension of surface tension, $\text{M}{{\text{L}}^{0}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}^{-2}}$.

concepts.title: Surface Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Surface Energy

 

If the area of the liquid surface has to be increased, work has to be done against the force of surface tension. The work done to form a film is stored as potential energy of the surface and the amount of the energy per unit are of this surface under isothermal condition is the intrinsic surface energy density or free surface energy density.

Work done in small displacement $\text{dx}$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & dW=F\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }dx=2TLdx \\ {} & W=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{x}2TLdx=2TLx \\ \end{array}$

As $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}=2\text{Lx}$ (area of both sides)

$\text{W}/\text{A}=\text{T}$ (intrinsic surface energy)

concepts.title: Excess Pressure
concepts.conceptBody:
Excess Pressure 

The pressure inside a soap bubble and outside it, are not identical due to the surface tension of the soap bubble. To calculate this pressure difference, let's first consider an air bubble inside a liquid. If the pressure differences is ${{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{\text{P}}}$, then the work done to increase the radius of bubble from $\text{r}$ to $\left( \text{r}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r} \right)$ is given by :

$\text{W}=\text{F }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r}=4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ p }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r}$

While change in area, $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ S}=4\pi {{(\text{r}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r})}^{2}}-4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}=8\pi \text{r }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r}$

From the definition of surface tension

$\text{T}=\text{W}/\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ S}$ $~=\frac{4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ p }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r}}{8\pi \text{r }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ r}}$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ p}$ $~=\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{r}}$

For a soap bubble in air, there are two surfaces, and so

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ p}=2\times 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}/\text{r}=\frac{4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{r}}$

concepts.title: Angle of Contact
concepts.conceptBody:
Angle of Contact 

  1. Angle of contact, for a solid and a liquid is defined as the angle between tangent of the liquid surface drawn at the point of contact and the surface inside the liquid.
  2. The angle of contact of a liquid surface on a solid surface depends on the nature of the liquid and the solid. 
     

Case I: When $\theta <9{{0}^{0}}$

The liquid surface curves up towards the solid. This happens when the force of cohension between two liquid molecules is less then the force of adhesion between the liquid and the solid. If such a liquid is poured into a solid tube, it will have a concave meniscus. For example, a glass rod dipped in water, or water inside a glass tube.

Case II: When $\theta >9{{0}^{0}}$

The liquid surfaces get curved downward in contact with a solid. In this case the force of cohension is greater than the force of adhesion. In such cases, solids do not get "wet". When such liquids are put into a solid tube, a convex meniscus is obtained. For example, a glass rod dipped in mercury or mercury within a solid glass tube.

concepts.title: Capillarity
concepts.conceptBody:
Capillarity 

Surface tension causes elevation or depression of the liquid in the narrow tube. This effect is called capillarity.

When a glass capillary (A tube of very small diameter is called a capillary tube) open at both ends is dipped vertically in water, the water in the tube will rise above the level of water in the vessel as shown in figure (a). In case of mercury, the liquid is depressed in the tube below the level of mercury in the vessel as shown in figure (b).

When the contact angle is less than ${{90}^{\circ }}$ the liquid rises in the tube. For a nonwetting liquid angle of contact is greater than ${{90}^{\circ }}$ and the surface is depressed, pulled down by the surface tension forces.

Explanation 

When a capillary tube dipped in water, the water meniscus inside the tube is concave. The pressure just below the meniscus is less than the pressure just above it by $\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{R}}$, where $\text{T}$ is the surface tension of the and $R$ is the radius of curvature of the meniscus. The pressure on the surface of water is ${{\text{P}}_{0}}$, the atmospheric pressure. The pressure just below the plane surface of water outside the tube is also ${{\text{P}}_{0}}$, but that just below the meniscus inside the tube is ${{\text{P}}_{0}}-\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{R}}$. We know that pressure at all points in the same level of water must be the same. Therefore, to make up the deficiency of pressure $\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{R}}$ below the meniscus water being $\text{s}$ to flow from outside into the tube. The rising of water in the capillary stops at a certain height $\text{h}$. In this position of pressure of water column of height $h$ becomes equal to $\frac{2T}{R}$, i.e.,

$\text{h}\rho \text{g}=\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{R}}$

Or $\text{h}=\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\text{R}\rho \text{g}}$

If $r$ is the radius of the capillary tube and $\theta $ the angle of contact, then

$\begin{matrix} \text{R}=\frac{2\text{r}}{\text{cos}\theta } \\ \text{h}=\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Tcos}\theta }{\text{r}\rho \text{g}} \\ \end{matrix}$
